data.title: Fed's Musalem open to another rate cut if further risks to job market emerge
data.description:
Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis President Alberto Musalem said Friday the U.S. central bank could consider another interest-rate cut if the labor market continues to weaken, while underscoring the need for a flexible, data-driven approach to monetary policy, with inflation still elevated.

“I could support a path with an additional reduction in the policy rate if there are further risks to the labor market that emerge,” Musalem said at an event in Washington, adding that further easing would also hinge on inflation staying in check.

“I do think we need to not be on a preset course,” he said, amid economic uncertainty. “Right now, I think it’s particularly important to go meeting by meeting.”

Last week, the St. Louis Fed chief said he would consider future rate reductions "to provide further assurance against labor-market weakening," while emphasizing the need to "tread with caution, because there's limited room for easing before monetary policy can become overly accommodative."

data.title: Truist targets 15% ROTCE by 2027 as revenue growth and buybacks accelerate
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Truist Financial Corporation (TFC) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO William Rogers highlighted a strategic investment in building 100 new branches in high-growth markets, renovating more than 300 locations, and enhancing digital capabilities. Rogers stated, "These new branches are designed for smarter client engagement with advanced AI-driven technology and dedicated premier adviser spaces all aimed at helping clients achieve financial success and further strengthening our presence in these dynamic communities."
  • Rogers reported, "For the third quarter, we reported net income available to common shareholders of $1.3 billion or $1.04 a share which included $0.02 a share of restructuring charges primarily related to severance." He emphasized the company's strong revenue performance, disciplined expense and credit management, and robust capital return, noting a 130 basis points sequential improvement in ROTCE to 13.6%.
  • Rogers described momentum across Consumer and Small Business Banking, with over 20,000 net new checking accounts and growth in balances, and stated, "Gen Z and Millennials represented 63% of this growth, a strong signal that our digital-first approach is resonating with the next generation of Truist clients."
  • Rogers said, "In Wholesale, I'm encouraged by this quarter's loan growth, improvement in investment banking and trading revenue and progress in key focus areas like payments and wealth."
  • CFO Michael Maguire added, "Adjusted revenue increased 3.7% linked quarter due to 9.9% growth in noninterest income and 1.2% growth in net interest income. Adjusted expenses increased 1% linked quarter, primarily due to higher personnel expenses related to incentives and strategic hiring efforts."

Outlook

  • Maguire stated, "Looking into the fourth quarter of 2025, we expect revenue to increase by approximately 1% to 2% relative to third quarter revenue of $5.2 billion. We expect net interest income to increase approximately 2% in the fourth quarter primarily driven by loan growth and lower deposit costs."
  • For full year 2025, management maintained its outlook for revenue growth of 1.5% to 2.5% and adjusted expense growth of approximately 1%. Maguire indicated, "We expect our effective tax rate to approximate 17.5% or 20% on a taxable equivalent basis in 2025."
  • Rogers projected, "We expect the rate of revenue growth in 2026 to more than double versus our growth rate this year. We also expect to generate more operating leverage in 2026 than we'll generate this year."
  • Rogers confirmed, "We're targeting a 15% ROTCE in 2027."

Financial Results

  • Net income available to common shareholders was $1.3 billion, or $1.04 per share, including $0.02 per share of restructuring charges.
  • Adjusted noninterest income increased 9.9% linked quarter to over $1.5 billion, driven by investment banking, trading, and wealth management income.
  • Average loan balances grew 2.5% linked quarter to $320 billion. Commercial loans saw a $4.8 billion, or 2.6%, increase.
  • Average deposits decreased $3.9 billion sequentially, primarily due to the withdrawal of $10.9 billion of short-term M&A-related deposits.
  • Asset quality metrics remained solid, with net charge-offs declining to 48 basis points and the ALLL ratio steady at 1.54% of total loans.
  • The company returned $1.2 billion of capital to shareholders through dividends and repurchased $500 million of common stock, with plans to target $750 million in share repurchases during the fourth quarter.

Q&A

  • John Pancari, Evercore: Asked for details on revenue growth components for 2026 and operating leverage. Rogers replied, "NII growth is a key part of our revenue... we'll have both loan and deposit growth, deposit growth better than this year and loan growth, probably a little more consistent."
  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler: Inquired about deposit pricing and buyback flexibility. Maguire answered, "We saw a touch of momentum in the third quarter... we thought we could get to sort of the mid-40s (beta) and that takes into consideration some of the momentum that we are seeing now."
  • Erika Penala, UBS: Questioned efficiency ratio targets and balancing investment. Rogers explained efficiency ratio will see marginal improvement, but "we're going to continue to invest... we're going to be driven by growth."
  • Kenneth Usdin, Bernstein: Asked about fixed rate loan repricing. Rogers responded, "That's going to be, I think, a nice tailwind for us in '26, but a diminishing tailwind with perhaps a long tail."
  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA: Sought a credit quality health check. Chief Risk Officer Brad Bender said, "The majority of our consumer portfolios are high-quality super-prime borrowers. We derisked in '23 and '24 on the lower end of the consumer spectrum."
  • Chris McGratty, KBW: Asked if First Brands exposure is reserved for. Bender confirmed, "We've got it accounted for in the forward guide."
  • Steven Alexopoulos, TD Cowen: Pressed on NDFI portfolio risk. Bender replied, "Today, we sit at about 11% of our total lending portfolio... well diversified, granular and then underweight relative."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone reflected cautious optimism, with recurring questions on deposit growth, loan pricing, capital return, credit quality, and NDFI exposures. Several questions pressed for greater detail on 2026 guidance and risk management amid industry events.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently citing "momentum" and "confidence" in strategic initiatives. Rogers repeatedly referenced being "optimistic" and "focused on executing with discipline."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed increased focus on forward-looking revenue acceleration, operating leverage, and risk exposures, especially regarding NDFI and credit issues.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for revenue and expense growth in 2025 remained unchanged; however, management provided substantially more detail on anticipated revenue acceleration and ROTCE targets for 2026 and 2027 in the current call.
  • Current quarter saw a shift in strategic focus to AI-driven branch investments and further digital engagement, while credit quality and risk diversification remained recurring analyst concerns.
  • Key metrics—like net income, loan growth, and noninterest income—showed sequential improvement compared to Q2, with greater emphasis on shareholder returns through expanded buyback plans.
  • Management’s tone was more assertively optimistic about achieving operating leverage and ROTCE targets than in the prior quarter.
  • Analysts’ tone grew slightly more skeptical, repeatedly questioning risk exposures.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged continued vigilance on credit risk, particularly NDFI exposures and recent market events. Rogers said, “The #1 risk for a bank is credit risk... we have been in the past. We're hypervigilant today and will be hypervigilant tomorrow.”
  • Bender confirmed ongoing monitoring of First Brands exposure, noting it is fully reserved and incorporated into guidance.
  • Potential for diminishing tailwinds from fixed rate loan repricing was discussed, with management indicating ongoing benefit but at a reduced pace.
  • Analyst concerns centered on deposit trends, loan growth sustainability, and how AI and digital investments will manifest in financial results.

Final Takeaway

Truist management underscored solid third quarter performance and announced aggressive investments in digital and branch expansion, with a clear pathway outlined to achieving a 15% ROTCE in 2027. Momentum in loan and deposit growth, digital engagement, and wealth management, combined with disciplined cost control and robust capital return, support confidence in stronger revenue growth and profitability targets for 2026 and beyond. Management remains vigilant on credit risks while emphasizing strategic investments and shareholder value creation as central to their long-term outlook.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wall Street searches for direction as investors navigate worries on regional banks' health
data.description:
U.S. stocks seesawed on Friday, as Wall Street was rattled by concerns on the health of regional banks and credit risks while the U.S. government shutdown entered its 17th day.

The major indexes wavered throughout Friday's session. The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.1%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was flat, and the Dow (DJI) was +0.2%.

“Markets are now paying attention to credit, and there are likely more skeletons in the closet of lenders. I am not sure yet whether this poses systemic risk, but the gaping jaw between business development companies and the S&P 500 will close one way or another,” said Chester Ntonifor, chief strategist at BCA Research, via X.com.

Stocks of investment bank Jefferies Financial (JEF) +4.2%, regional banks Zions Bancorporation (ZION) +4.1%, and Western Alliance (WAL) +1.8% were initially lower on Friday as investors worried about the quality of loans and investments made by the firms, but have since recovered. Goldman Sachs (GS) and J.P. Morgan (JPM), however, are some of the top decliners in the Dow (DJI).

The KBW Nasdaq Regional Banking Index (KRX) closed down 6.3% on Thursday trading, its biggest drop since April, following the disclosures by two banks of irregularities related to some loans -- last +1.1%.

Meanwhile, the U.S. government shutdown continued to grind on as it entered its third week, after a GOP funding bill failed for the 10th time. Prediction market Kalshi now sees the government shutdown lasting almost 40 days, the longest in history.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 4 basis points to 4.01%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 3 basis points to 3.46%.

“The bond market is pricing in 2 more 25 bps rate cuts by year-end and 3 more 25 bps cuts in 2026. That would bring the Fed Funds Rate below 3%. After a brief hiatus, easy money is back,” said Charlie Bilello, chief market strategist at Creative Planning.

Wall Street's major averages closed lower on Thursday amid increased volatility due to the ongoing U.S.-China trade conflict and bad loan reports in banks, while investors continued to assess earnings reports and the ongoing U.S. government shutdown.

data.title: Progyny seesaws amid confusion over impact from Trump’s IVF initiative
data.description:
Shares of fertility benefits provider Progyny (NASDAQ:PGNY) turned volatile as the Trump administration announced a new initiative to expand access to in vitro fertilization alongside a major drug pricing deal with fertility treatment maker EMD Serono of Merck KGaA (OTCPK:MKGAF).

Progyny (NASDAQ:PGNY) gained sharply before closing ~5% higher on Thursday as the New York Times reported about the upcoming announcement ahead of its launch by President Donald Trump at the White House later that day.

The stock fell ~10% on Friday as Wall Street analysts weighed in on EMD Serono’s deal with the Trump administration, under which the company has agreed to offer its IVF therapies at steep discounts to cash-paying patients through the government’s direct-to-consumer site, TrumpRx.

“The volatility in PGNY shares, including the move higher intraday on Thursday and the pre-market weakness Friday, shows the confusion about how to read this fertility push for PGNY,” Leerink analyst Michael Cherny wrote, according to Bloomberg News.

As part of the agreement, Merck KGaA (MKGAY) became the third leading drugmaker after Pfizer (PFE) and AstraZeneca (AZN) to lower U.S. prices of their products in line with those in other developed countries under Trump’s most-favored-nation drug pricing policy.

Cherny, with an Outperform rating on Progyny (NASDAQ:PGNY), argued that “popular feedback has been negative, with concerns that the lower cash-pay price will erode PGNY’s Rx revenue, that is roughly 1/3 of company with similar gross margins.”

Additionally, analysts noted the administration’s efforts to establish a new benefit option through which employers will be able to deliver fertility benefits directly to employees as part of standalone benefit packages.

Leerink said it is not yet convinced how the initiative will impact PGNY’s value proposition, but added that “the support of carve-out benefits as more likely to benefit PGNY LT than disrupt the model.”

Truist analyst Jailendra Singh argued that expansion of fertility benefits among small and midsize employers is “broadly viewed as a net positive for Progyny (and the broader fertility industry) as this direction aligns with Progyny’s existing roadmap.”

The company “emphasizes that “Most Favored Nation” and TrumpRx pricing initiatives apply only to cash-pay patients, specifically underinsured or uninsured individuals,” Singh wrote with a Hold rating on PGNY.

data.title: Deutsche Bank loan book stays sound after First Brands, Tricolor blowups, CEO says
data.description:
At a time when confidence across the credit space is being tested after recent blowups, Deutsche Bank (NYSE:DB) CEO Christian Sewing said Friday the German lender has seen no signs of credit deterioration in its loan book.

“There is no deterioration, we’re very confident with our credit portfolio,” Deutsche Bank CEO Christian Sewing said in an interview on Bloomberg Television Friday.

The recent failures of auto lender Tricolor Holdings and First Brands Group -- the auto-parts supplier with over $10B in liabilities have left some major Wall Street creditors exposed, including Jefferies Financial Group (JEF) and UBS Group (UBS).

JPMorgan Chase (JPM), took a $170M charge-off related to its Tricolor exposure, which CEO Jamie Dimon publicly called "not our finest moment" and also used a waterbug reference. "When you see one cockroach, there are probably more," he declared, before many more banks revealed additional fallout.

DB shares slid 4.3% in afternoon trading in the U.S.

Meanwhile, U.S. bank stocks started the climb back up after suffering a jolt down on Thursday, as bank commentary and analysts pointed out that banks' fundamentals appear to be generally solid, and the instances of credit risk events aren't representative of the broader sector.

data.title: Deere upgraded to Buy at UBS, with ag machinery downcycle seen near its end
data.description:
Deere (NYSE:DE) +0.2% in choppy trading Friday as UBS upgraded shares to Buy from Neutral with a $535 price target, expecting 2026 to be the last year of the company's earnings downturn before a recovery in 2027.

"We believe Ag fundamentals and sentiment are near the bottom, and while it may be a bit early for the stock today based on elevated expectations for '26... a positive inflection in the cycle amd DE earnings should begin to be priced in over the next year," UBS analyst Steven Fisher wrote, estimating Deere's (NYSE:DE) sales coming in 4% lower in 2026 but 13% higher in 2027, and "as the inflection in earnings becomes more visible, we expect estimates to be revised higher."

Fisher sees Deere’s (DE) FY 2026 earnings remaining under pressure, with earnings forecast at $17.90/share, down from $20/share previously, as agricultural fundamentals and sentiment continue to stabilize, before expecting a recovery in FY 2027, with EPS rising to $23.20, supported by improving farm incomes, dealer restocking, and easing interest rates.

The analyst noted North American sales of large tractors are near historical troughs, and commodity prices, particularly corn and soybeans, are near multi-year lows, providing further upside potential if prices rebound.

data.title: Glacier Bancorp signals continued margin growth into 2026 while expanding in Texas through $3.1B Guaranty acquisition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Glacier Bancorp (GBCI) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Randall Chesler highlighted "strong margin expansion, higher loan yields, lower deposit costs and solid high-quality loan growth" in the quarter. Glacier Bancorp completed the core conversion of Bank of Idaho, integrating assets of approximately $1.4 billion, and closed the $3.1 billion acquisition of Guaranty Bank & Trust, marking its entry into Texas. Chesler stated, "Our focus now is on delivering a flawless conversion in the first quarter of 2026 and making sure we have happy employees and customers."
  • Net income for the third quarter was $67.9 million, or $0.57 per diluted share. This represented "an increase of 29% from the prior quarter and reflects a 33% increase in net income compared to the same quarter last year."
  • Pretax pre-provision net revenues for the first nine months reached $250 million, increasing $77.1 million or 45% over the prior year period. The loan portfolio grew by $258 million to $18.8 billion, a 6% annualized growth from the prior quarter. Deposits rose to $22 billion, up 4% annualized, with noninterest-bearing deposits now at 31% of total deposits.
  • CFO Ron Copher reported, "Noninterest expense was $168 million, up $13 million or 8% from the second quarter, primarily due to increased costs from acquisitions. Noninterest income totaled $35 million in the current quarter, up $2.4 million or 7% from the prior quarter and up 2% year-over-year." Copher also stated, "We declared our 162nd consecutive quarterly dividend of $0.33 per share."

Outlook

  • Senior VP & Treasurer Byron Pollan indicated ongoing margin expansion: "For Q4, we anticipate that, that will grow. Our margin, additional 18 to 20 basis points in the fourth quarter, that does include the impact of Guaranty." Pollan added, "What I can say is that we do expect to see continued margin growth throughout the year. I would say, though, that the pace of quarterly increase is likely to moderate throughout next year."
  • On the expense side, Copher said, "with the acquisition of Guaranty Bank on October 1... we're now going to have a full 3 months of expense from Guaranty, and this will cause a step up in our core noninterest expense. It will add $21 million to $22 million to core noninterest expense in the fourth quarter."

Financial Results

  • Net interest income was reported at $225 million, up $18 million or 9% from the prior quarter and up $45 million or 25% from the same quarter last year. The net interest margin on a tax adjusted basis expanded to 3.39%, up 18 basis points from the prior quarter.
  • Total earning asset yield in the current quarter increased to 4.86%. Total cost of funding declined to 1.58%. Noninterest expense was $168 million, with $7 million in acquisition-related expense and $800,000 in fixed asset write-downs. The efficiency ratio remained at 62%.
  • Credit quality was characterized as "very strong," with nonperforming assets at 0.19% of total assets and net charge-offs at $2.9 million for the quarter. Allowance for credit remains at 1.22% of total loans.

Q&A

  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Asked about margin trajectory and visibility, Pollan answered, "We do see continued growth ahead of us in terms of our outlook. For Q4, we anticipate that, that will grow. Our margin, additional 18 to 20 basis points in the fourth quarter, that does include the impact of Guaranty."
  • Rulis followed up on Texas market expansion, Chesler responded, "Guaranty may be the best cultural fit of any acquisition we've done in the last 10 years... our focus right now is on getting Guaranty converted in 1Q and making sure that goes extremely well."
  • David Feaster, Raymond James: Inquired about loan growth and competition. Tom Dolan, Chief Credit Officer, said, "from a pipeline perspective, we continue to see consistent pull-through. We continue to see consistent build back."
  • Feaster asked about credit risks, Dolan explained, "The only industry that I would say is a little bit outsized is probably the ag sector... But outside of that, there's really no specific geography or industry segment that's showing an outsized level of risk."
  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler: Sought clarity on deposit costs and beta, Pollan replied, "Our spot deposit cost on September 30 was 1.22%. In terms of our beta, to this point, we've been able to achieve a down rate beta somewhere in the mid-teens."
  • Andrew Terrell, Stephens: Probed about expense run rate and M&A cost saves, Copher said, "we're modeling 20% reduction in noninterest expense cost saves. 50% of that, we will achieve in '26. The other 50% will be in '27."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts posed probing questions on margin sustainability, deposit cost trajectory, credit quality, and M&A integration, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone, with interest in forward guidance and operational integration.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone during prepared remarks, with Pollan stating, "we do expect to see continued margin growth throughout the year," and Chesler describing the Guaranty acquisition as "the best cultural fit of any acquisition we've done in the last 10 years." During Q&A, management responses remained thorough and cautiously optimistic, signaling awareness of potential moderation in margin increases and expense impacts.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management demonstrated increased confidence in margin expansion and integration progress, with analysts maintaining their focus on growth drivers and cost controls.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance on margin expansion increased from a 15-17 basis point range in Q2 to an 18-20 basis point range for Q4 in Q3, with acknowledgment that the pace may moderate in 2026.
  • Strategic focus shifted from integrating the Bank of Idaho to executing the Guaranty Bank & Trust acquisition and Texas market entry.
  • Loan and deposit growth remained strong, with continued emphasis on commercial real estate and noninterest-bearing deposits.
  • Noninterest expense guidance was raised to reflect the impact of acquisitions, and management projected significant cost saves post-conversion.
  • Management's tone in Q3 was more confident about growth prospects, while analysts continued to focus on cost structure and integration challenges.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified the agricultural sector as an area of heightened risk but expressed confidence in asset security and lender experience.
  • Analysts raised questions about integration risks, expense run rates, margin sustainability, and credit normalization.
  • Dolan emphasized that "outside of [ag], there's really no specific geography or industry segment that's showing an outsized level of risk."
  • Management underscored a conservative approach to credit and ongoing expense discipline.

Final Takeaway

Glacier Bancorp reported a strong third quarter with notable margin expansion, solid loan and deposit growth, and successful execution of recent acquisitions. Management expects continued, though moderating, margin growth into 2026, underpinned by disciplined expense management, a conservative credit posture, and the strategic expansion into Texas. The outlook remains positive as the company integrates Guaranty Bank & Trust and continues to pursue organic and acquisition-driven growth across its expanding footprint.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Market Voices: Moody's on credit markets; Trump on China, Ozempic
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements, and remarks that could impact markets, sectors, or individual stocks.

  • Moody's head of global private credit, Marc Pinto, said he doesn't see a banking crisis looming despite increased investor concern about the quality of certain bank loans.

“When we dig deeper here and look to see if there’s a turn in the credit cycle, which is effectively what the market seems to be focusing on, we can find no evidence,” Pinto told CNBC. “That could always change. But if we look at the asset quality numbers that we’ve seen over the last several quarters, we’re seeing very little deterioration at all.”

Pinto also noted that the U.S. economy has proved to be more resilient than expected.

“With respect to GDP growth, we’re doing much better than many people thought just six months ago,” he said. “So again, the credit conditions, looking at GDP growth as well as an expected decline in interest rates, we feel the credit quality is in a pretty good place today and potentially may improve.”

  • President Trump said the high tariffs he has threatened to impose on imports from China, which could run as high as 157%, are not sustainable in the long run. 

"It's not sustainable, but that's what the number is," Trump told Fox Business. "It's probably not, you know, it could stand. But they forced me to do that."

Trump confirmed that he planned to meet with Chinese President Xi in South Korea "in a couple of weeks."

  • Trump also said he sees the cost of Novo Nordisk's (NVO) diabetes drug Ozempic, which is marketed for weight loss under the name Wegovy, falling to $150 a month "out of pocket."

The president spoke about lowering the cost of the drug, which runs around $1,000 a month, during a press conference on Thursday, according to Bloomberg.

Novo Nordisk's Ozempic belongs to a class of drugs known as GLP-1s, which also includes Eli Lilly's (LLY) diabetes drug Mounjaro, which is marketed for weight loss as Zepbound.

data.title: Rio Tinto ramps up Simandou stockpiles to 2M tons for first iron ore shipment - Reuters
data.description:
Rio Tinto (NYSE:RIO) has stockpiled 2 million metric tons of high-grade iron ore at its giant Simandou project in Guinea for an inaugural shipment in mid-November, which is expected to head to China, Reuters reported Friday.

In its recent Q3 production report, Rio (NYSE:RIO) said SimFer - one of the two mines at Simandou - had stockpiled 1.5 million tons of ore, with first ore loaded on rail transport in October.

Simandou, which holds an estimated 4 billion tons of ore averaging 65% iron content, is expected to deliver 120 million tons/year at full capacity, with SimFer contributing half.

"Iron ore prices could face downward pressure if Australian and Brazilian miners don't respond to Simandou's ramp-up," Panmure Liberum head of commodities Tom Price said in a note. "A 120 million tons annual output by 2028 would lift seaborne supply by 8%-9%."

Ownership of Simandou, which is linked to the Atlantic via a 600 km railway and deepwater port, is split between a consortium of Rio (RIO) and Chinese state-owned Chalco and Winning Consortium Simandou, a Singapore-China consortium.

data.title: Cohen & Steers signals $1.75B pipeline and expects 2025 comp ratio to remain at 40.25% amid private real estate and ETF expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cohen & Steers (CNS) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Joseph Harvey, CEO, described the quarter as one of solid financial results and positive flows, highlighting that "our institutional pipeline built up meaningfully. New strategies and vehicles are gaining traction, and we are making progress on distribution initiatives." Harvey also announced the successful pricing of an equity rights offering for the Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF), raising $353 million, which combined with leverage provides "over $500 million in dry powder to allocate to opportunities in global infrastructure such as increased power demand and decarbonization, the digital transformation of economies and deglobalization with transforming supply chains."
  • Harvey explained that net inflows of $233 million brought year-to-date inflows to $325 million, with "net inflows of $768 million into open-end funds and net outflows of $455 million and $82 million from institutional advisory and subadvisory respectively."
  • Harvey also provided a management change update: "I'll close by thanking Raja Dakkuri for his service to Cohen & Steers and wish him all the best in his new opportunity. Mike Donohue will take on the role of interim CFO; and Brian Meta as Head of FP&A and IR, will continue to facilitate communications with you."
  • Raja Dakkuri, CFO, stated, "Yesterday, we reported earnings of $0.81 per share compared to $0.73 sequentially, representing an increase of 11.6% versus Q2." Dakkuri emphasized, "Revenue for Q3 increased 4.2% from the prior quarter to $141 million. The change in revenue from the prior quarter was driven by higher average AUM, plus an additional day during the period."
  • Dakkuri noted operating margin growth, "Our operating margin increased meaningfully to 36.1% compared to 33.6% in Q2."
  • Dakkuri detailed liquidity and AUM, stating, "Our liquidity totaled $364 million at quarter end which compares positively to $323 million in the prior quarter" and "ending AUM increased to $90.9 billion as of Q3."
  • John Cheigh, President & Chief Investment Officer, highlighted, "Our 1, 3 and 5-year excess returns of 184 basis points, 227 basis points and 216 basis points, respectively, are near or above our targets." Cheigh also celebrated, "Our real estate ETF has outperformed by 217 basis points since inception and is #1 versus its peers."

Outlook

  • Dakkuri provided guidance: "With respect to comp and benefits for 2025, we expect our compensation ratio to remain at 40.25% for the full year. We expect full year G&A increase of around 9% compared to full year 2024."
  • He added, "In 2026, we expect annual G&A changes to moderate from this year's growth level to being in the mid-single-digit percentage range. Lastly, we expect our effective tax rate to remain at 25.1% on an as-adjusted basis."

Financial Results

  • Dakkuri reported, "Revenue for Q3 increased 4.2% from the prior quarter to $141 million."
  • He stated, "Our effective fee rate was 59 basis points, in line with the prior quarter. Our operating margin increased meaningfully to 36.1% compared to 33.6% in Q2."
  • Ending AUM rose to $90.9 billion as of Q3, and liquidity reached $364 million.
  • "Total expenses during Q3 were essentially flat to the prior quarter due to several drivers. G&A expenses decreased meaningfully versus the prior quarter...compensation and benefits increased during the quarter, the change in comp and benefits was below the change in revenue. As a result, our compensation ratio for the quarter was lower."

Q&A

  • John Dunn, Evercore ISI: The demand for U.S. REITs in the wealth management channel has been good lately. Can you maybe compare how that's developed versus past cycles leading up to interest rates? Has it been slower to materialize? Has it been about the same? And then do you think flows can -- in the wealth channel can accelerate from where they are in the past few months? Joseph Harvey responded, "I would say that the progression of the interest rate cycle this time, which has been about as extreme as we've ever seen it...has created a different dynamic and that real estate pricing had really advanced with -- in the 0 interest rate environment. And as rates have normalized real estate pricing has had to adjust. We think that's mostly occurred. But in some sectors it still needs to happen." John Cheigh added, "we expect REIT earnings to accelerate into 2026 and 2027."
  • Dunn: On the institutional side, any flavor of who's giving you money geographically or client-type profile? Any other areas besides U.S. REITs in particular that people are putting money to work? Harvey replied, "it's predominantly North America, and it's a wide variety of investors, including retirement plans for individuals, annuity providers...one interesting non-U.S. allocation we've seen recently...was from a European institution who...added about half of what they redeemed into a European real estate strategy."
  • Rodrigo Ferreira, BofA Securities: As rates continue to go down, where do you expect that cash sitting on the sidelines to go into? And which of your strategies do you feel stand to benefit the most from a flows perspective? Harvey answered, "I would expect as Jon laid out, the big picture that allocations to and diversifiers to real asset strategies that are inflation sensitive probably should be going up. So that would point to our real estate strategies, our infrastructure strategies and our multi-strategy real assets portfolios."
  • Ferreira: On comp ratio in 2026 and longer term, how do you think about investing in the business versus opportunity to continue to expand operating margin? Harvey replied, "so far this year, we've had decent revenue growth, and that appreciation is the best formula to help the comp ratio and, of course, the associated margin impact...as we launch more [active ETFs], that will include some additional expenses, but we will scale this up and we expect it to be additive at the margin."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on demand trends, geographic flows, and margin sustainability, with a pragmatic interest in strategic execution and future growth.
  • Management’s sentiment was confident regarding business momentum, pipeline strength, and new product traction, as evidenced by statements like "we believe we have transitioned to a net positive position on the institutional flow front" and "we are on track to launch 2 more ETFs in the fourth quarter."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst questions reflected more optimism about future flows and margin leverage. Management’s confidence increased, especially in new mandates, pipeline size, and product launches.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q3 saw a stronger rebound in net inflows ($233 million vs. Q2 net outflows of $131 million), with a substantially increased won but unfunded pipeline ($1.75 billion vs. $776 million in Q2).
  • Operating margin rose to 36.1% from 33.6% in Q2, while liquidity improved from $323 million to $364 million.
  • Management guidance for compensation ratio was slightly reduced to 40.25% from 40.5%.
  • Strategic focus shifted toward scaling active ETFs and expanding private real estate offerings, with the successful UTF rights offering as a notable capital-raising event.
  • Management’s tone was more optimistic about future flows, new mandates, and margin expansion, while analyst tone moved from cautious to more constructive.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited sector-specific adjustments in real estate pricing and acknowledged that "in some sectors it still needs to happen."
  • Harvey noted institutional net outflows were mainly due to "European client derisking their U.S. allocation in favor of international and one...due to the restructuring of a retirement plan."
  • The company remains focused on expense management but expects "G&A increase of around 9% compared to full year 2024," driven by earlier-year investments in talent and business development.
  • Harvey mentioned potential ongoing headwinds from the perceived volatility of listed REITs versus private real estate, despite historical outperformance.

Final Takeaway

Cohen & Steers delivered a quarter marked by revenue and margin expansion, a significant build in the institutional pipeline, and progress in both active ETF and private real estate initiatives. Management highlighted a transition to net positive institutional flows, a disciplined approach to expenses, and strong conviction in the long-term opportunity for real assets and infrastructure. The firm reinforced its optimism for continued growth through new product launches and strategic capital deployment, underscored by a robust liquidity position and a stable compensation outlook for the remainder of 2025.
